ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yong Tau Foo with Laksa combination. Mapped to generic noodle dish as closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — Southeast Asian style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yong Tau Foo Laksa. A0FJP (noodle soup) used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for this coconut-milk noodle soup combination dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish combination.

main_ingredients: rice vermicelli (bee hoon) or yellow noodles

serving.description: 1 set meal (1 bowl Laksa with noodles/rice + 8 pcs Yong Tau Foo)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal consists of a bowl of laksa noodle soup (approximately 400–450 g) paired with 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo items (approximately 250–300 g), totalling around 700 g.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Laksa and Yong Tau Foo component values
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for Laksa (coconut milk-based) and Yong Tau Foo (boiled/fried items with fish paste). The input per_100g values (energy 135.71 kcal, protein 6.43 g, fat 9.29 g, carbohydrate 7.14 g) are consistent with HPB Foodo reference ranges for this combination dish. Values retained and cross-referenced against IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Laksa components. Weighted composite per 100 g of the 700 g set meal used. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat not separately reported in primary source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli and laksa noodle analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with Laksa as a combined dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for rice vermicelli (bee hoon) GI ~58 and coconut milk laksa broth which lowers effective GI due to fat and protein content. Weighted estimate accounts for 8 pcs YTF (predominantly protein/fat, negligible carbohydrate contribution) and the laksa noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (50 g from 700 g × 7.14 g/100 g) / 100 = 26.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g; 2800 mg per 700 g serving exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced laksa paste and avoid adding fish sauce or chilli sauce at the table, as a single 700 g serving delivers approximately 2800 mg sodium — well above the 2000 mg daily target; choosing a smaller portion or sharing the set meal is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~52) and moderate carbohydrate (7.14 g/100 g; ~50 g per serving) with glycemic load of 26 per serving; coconut milk broth is calorie-dense
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (~52) and moderate carbohydrate content support glycaemic stability when portion size is controlled; individuals with type 2 diabetes should select thick rice vermicelli over yellow noodles to further reduce GI, limit the noodle portion, and be mindful that the calorie-dense coconut milk broth contributes significantly to total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g (35 g per 700 g serving) from coconut milk, combined with sodium at 400 mg/100 g (2800 mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from coconut milk (5.0 g/100 g; ~35 g per serving) and elevated sodium (2800 mg per serving) represent dual cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a lighter coconut milk ratio, limit portion size, and avoid this dish as a regular meal choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g from coconut milk (pro-inflammatory); sodium at 400 mg/100 g; nightshade ingredients (brinjal, chilli) present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat below 5 g/100 g, be cautious of nightshade ingredients (brinjal, chilli) which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals, while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of turmeric and lemongrass in the laksa paste.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Laksa (8 pieces) is a popular Singaporean combination set meal consisting of eight pieces of Yong Tau Foo — tofu and vegetables stuffed with seasoned fish paste — served alongside a bowl of rich, spicy Laksa noodle soup. Yong Tau Foo items typically include bitter gourd, brinjal, chilli, tofu puffs, and fish cake, prepared by boiling or deep-frying before being added to the dish. The Laksa component features rice vermicelli (bee hoon) or yellow noodles submerged in a fragrant coconut milk-based curry broth made with laksa paste, lemongrass, turmeric, and galangal, garnished with bean sprouts, prawns, and fish cake. The flavour profile is rich, creamy, and spicy with savoury umami depth from the fish paste and broth. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 9.3 g fat (of which 5.0 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 7.1 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g, making the full 700 g serving a high-sodium meal (~2800 mg). The estimated glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), supported by the fat and protein content of the broth slowing glucose absorption. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat load, and of moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to the calorie-dense broth and moderate glycemic load of ~26 per serving. It is a culturally significant hawker dish widely available across Singapore and Malaysia, commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo reference ranges for Laksa and Yong Tau Foo components and cross-referenced against IMR Malaysia MyFCD. However, no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific 8-piece YTF + Laksa combination dish; values are adapted from component-level data. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this Yong Tau Foo with Laksa combination dish is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish combination not available in HPB Foodo or MyFCD as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish not available in primary approved sources as of 2026. trans_fat_g value of 0.07 g/100 g retained from input; this trace level is consistent with minor trans fat contributions from deep-frying of YTF items. Input data source was listed as grabfood (a forbidden primary source); nutrient values have been cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD and found to be within plausible ranges for this dish type — values retained with medium confidence. The dish is a Singaporean hawker combination meal; primary cuisine classification is Singaporean / Malaysian Chinese. Halal status is generally applicable at certified hawker stalls but may vary by vendor.
